items.title_original: LibreOffice 26.4 Beta Experiments with AI Writing Features
items.title_ru: LibreOffice 26.4 Beta экспериментирует с AI-функциями для письма
items.description_ru: Бета-версия LibreOffice тестирует инструменты AI-помощи для редактирования и улучшения текстов.

raw.title: LibreOffice 26.4 Beta Experiments with AI Writing Features
raw.content: LibreOffice 26.4 Beta Experiments with AI Writing Features and Smarter Editing Tools | Linux Journal Skip to main content #Linux #LibreOffice #Beta LibreOffice 26.4 Beta Experiments with AI Writing Features and Smarter Editing Tools by George Whittaker on May 5, 2026 The upcoming LibreOffice 26.4 Beta is introducing early AI-powered writing capabilities, signaling a new direction for the open-source office suite. While LibreOffice has traditionally focused on privacy, local processing, and open standards, the beta release shows that The Document Foundation is now exploring how artificial intelligence can assist users without fully embracing cloud-dependent ecosystems. The result is a cautious but notable step toward AI-enhanced productivity on Linux and other desktop platforms. AI Writing Assistance Comes to LibreOffice One of the biggest additions connected to LibreOffice 26.4 Beta is expanded support for AI-assisted writing tools through integrations such as WritingTool , an open-source LibreOffice extension designed to enhance editing workflows. These AI features focus on practical writing assistance rather than aggressive automation. Current capabilities include: Grammar and style suggestions Paragraph rewriting and refinement Text expansion and summarization Translation assistance AI-assisted content generation Unlike many proprietary AI platforms, these tools can operate using local AI models , allowing users to avoid sending documents to external cloud services. A Privacy-Focused Approach to AI LibreOffice’s AI direction differs from the strategies used by many commercial office suites. Instead of tightly integrating mandatory cloud AI services, the project appears focused on: Optional AI functionality User-controlled integrations Support for local inference servers Compatibility with self-hosted AI solutions The WritingTool project specifically highlights support for local AI backends and OpenAI-compatible APIs, including self-hosted tools like LocalAI. This approach aligns closely with the values of many Linux and open-source users who prioritize privacy and transparency. What AI Tools Can Actually Do The AI writing features currently being tested are aimed at improving productivity rather than replacing human writing entirely. Examples include: Grammar and Style Improvements AI can analyze text for readability, awkward phrasing, and stylistic consistency. Paragraph Rewriting Users can ask the assistant to: Simplify text Make writing more formal or casual Expand short sections Rephrase unclear sentences Content Assistance The tools can also help generate outlines, draft paragraphs, or suggest alternative wording for documents. Multilingual Support Translation and language refinement features are being expanded to support international workflows. LibreOffice Remains Open Source Importantly, LibreOffice itself remains fully open source and community-driven. The introduction of AI features does not appear to change the suite’s core philosop

raw.summary: Welcome to Memia #2026.18 … another weekly superscan across what’s been happening at the frontier of AI, emerging tech and the accelerating future. As always so much going on, thanks for reading! ℹ️PSA: Memia sends *very long emails*, best viewed online or in the Substack app . This newsletter is no...
raw.content: Memia #2026.18: planetary solvency🌍📊 goblin mode👺 runtime governance🏦▶️ redundant biomass🏰💀 agent sprawl🤖 war-damaged cloud💥☁️ canary traps🐤 quadsqueezing⚛️ exbodied intelligence🐜 Subscribe Sign in Memia Newsletter Memia #2026.18: planetary solvency🌍📊 goblin mode👺 runtime governance🏦▶️ redundant biomass🏰💀 agent sprawl🤖 war-damaged cloud💥☁️ canary traps🐤 quadsqueezing⚛️ exbodied intelligence🐜 Apocalypse early warning system🛩️🛩️ Ben Reid May 06, 2026 Share Welcome to Memia #2026.18 … another weekly superscan across what’s been happening at the frontier of AI, emerging tech and the accelerating future. As always so much going on, thanks for reading! ℹ️PSA: Memia sends *very long emails*, best viewed online or in the Substack app . This newsletter is now almost entirely produced using Memia Sensorium , this week using AI agents to ingest 52+ technology news feeds, scan 2339+ articles and curate / collate / summarise ~204 future signals below. (Responding to feedback from a few folks over the last couple of months…) Yes, I *KNOW* that these emails are now too long to be read by most people each week! The challenge is that news volumes continue to increase so my tactic is to leverage AI to keep the net wide to have any hope of catching signal in the noise… As I discussed at the beginning of the year , one outcome of the newsletter being increasingly compiled by me^AI is that I expect the audience will morph into subscribers who use their AI agents to do the pre-processing for them. (Coming soon, for example… Memia as JSON object. Are we keeping up yet?!😵‍💫) So, top tip for now: paste this whole email into your Obsidian/Roam knowledge graph (…or save in a file if you’re old school…) and get your AI agent of choice to search / notify / summarise / further research the bits you are most interested in. (As always, if this isn’t for you any more, please feel free to unsubscribe at any time using the link at the footer of this email, thank you for being along for the ride so far.) AI, so: expect errors, hallucinations and occasional “it’s not an X, it’s a Y” (plus goblins )… Please click through to read the original linked articles that interest you - images are generally those included in the RSS syndication feeds of the articles being linked to. To keep up with the latest signals that Memia is tracking in real time, you can follow the combined Sensorium Feed at https://www.memia.com/insights/signals/ … Memia is an ad-free, reader-supported service, running mostly on learning endorphins and high-quality caffeine☕. If you value these weekly tech uploads in your inbox please shout me a monthly coffee by upgrading to a paid subscription. Only US$5/month or US$50 per year (or thereabouts in your local currency). Thank you!🙏 Subscribe 🗞️Weekly roundup The most clicked link in last week’s newsletter was: US authorities freeze $344M in crypto linked to Iran . (…On which topic: Blockchain intelligence firm Nominis reported that the wallets' structural and behaviour

As always, thanks for reading! (Or… getting your AI agent to read). (Apologies for running late hitting “send” (again)… I spent all yesterday...
raw.content: Memia #2026.17: Wet vs. dry🌊🧠🏜️ triple-triple⚡️📉 copy.fail⚠️ gpt-5.5📈 deepseek v4🐋 irony, distilled⚗️ bullshitbench v2🐮💩 ipv8?🌐🚧 proof-of-reserves🪙✅ hydrovoltaic🌊⚡ false vacuum decay⚛️💥🫧 Subscribe Sign in Memia Newsletter Memia #2026.17: Wet vs. dry🌊🧠🏜️ triple-triple⚡️📉 copy.fail⚠️ gpt-5.5📈 deepseek v4🐋 irony, distilled⚗️ bullshitbench v2🐮💩 ipv8?🌐🚧 proof-of-reserves🪙✅ hydrovoltaic🌊⚡ false vacuum decay⚛️💥🫧 Human Maladaptation Syndrome🌍💀 Ben Reid Apr 30, 2026 Share Welcome to Memia #2026.17 … another weekly megascan across what’s happening around the world at the frontier of AI, emerging tech and the accelerating future. As always, thanks for reading! (Or… getting your AI agent to read). (Apologies for running late hitting “send” (again)… I spent all yesterday on budget long-haul flights heading home with no in-flight wifi and even less elbow room… Finally made it back to Aotearoa last night, good to be home !) 👓Heads-up I spent the last leg of my trip in Thailand visiting my friend Henry Lane 1 hour up the coast from Phuket near the small town of Khao Lak. Henry and his wife Wawwa have spent the last few years building up a tourism and hospitality group including Valhalla - their beachside cafe which has seriously world-beating views for digital nomad life: Vibing the new website in (air-conditioned) paradise Henry has a background in VR / AR (he was one of the earliest VR application developers with Corvecto ) … these days he’s working with Even Realities glasses to help him run the business - a non-intrusive headsup display enabling seamless interactions with backend business systems using voice recognition and realtime notifications. (Amazing what you can stitch together with Claude Code these days…) The G1 spectacles (pictured, doesn’t quite do the UX justice) are version 1, but the headsup text display really does work (and completely unobtrusive - I didn’t notice he was wearing them until he told me…). Also open source support from MentraOS SDK including realtime translation. The upgraded Even G2 model has just been released.
